nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-moving freshwater bodies including ponds, ditches, paddies/rice fields, and the shallow margins of lakes and slow rivers

decorPreferences: any substrate or no substrate (tolerates floating)
dense planted thickets or surface mats for fry and shrimp refuge

summary: Indian Naiad (Najas indica), also sold as Guppy Grass, is a fast-growing fine-leaved stem plant from Asian still-waters, widely used as fry and shrimp refuge in breeding and planted aquariums.
careNotes: Indian Naiad is a fast-growing annual aquatic plant from still or slow-moving freshwaters of India, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, and New Guinea. In the aquarium trade it is most often sold under the names 'Guppy Grass' — a name shared with the New-World Najas guadalupensis; the two are often used interchangeably by retailers, and the plant sold as 'Guppy Grass' or 'Najas Roraima' is not always identified to species — and 'Indian Naiad'. It can be grown either as a loose stem plant pushed gently into substrate or simply left floating, and both forms generally produce dense thickets within weeks under low-to-moderate light without CO2 supplementation. Water temperatures around 22–28 °C are generally recommended; the species tends to tolerate a wide range of pH (6.0–7.5) and water hardness, and adapts readily to most aquarium conditions, making it a common recommendation for beginner and breeding setups. Stems are brittle and snap easily, which is the main propagation route: any broken fragment with a node will continue growing. This same brittleness is what makes Indian Naiad excellent fry and shrimp refuge — the dense, tangled, fine-leaved mass provides countless hiding spaces for newborn livebearers, killifish, and shrimplets. INVASIVE RISK: this species has established itself in waterways outside its native range, notably in parts of the Mediterranean and southern Europe. Aquarists should never release trimmings or unwanted plant material into local waterways, and should dispose of cuttings in household waste or compost. No fertilisation, special substrate, or CO2 is required for healthy growth; supplemental fertilisation will increase the growth rate but is rarely necessary. The plant feeds from the water column rather than the substrate. Regular trimming is generally needed to prevent it from overtaking a tank.
breedingNotes: Indian Naiad propagates readily by stem fragmentation — any cutting or broken fragment with at least one node will continue growing whether floated or planted. Lateral side-shoots from established stems can also be separated and replanted. Because the stems are brittle, propagation often happens spontaneously as a side effect of routine maintenance.

Article is a stub and contains no husbandry-specific data.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: low-to-moderate light, no CO2 needed, fast growth, can be planted or left floating, height 4–12 in (≈10–30 cm), pH 6.0–7.5, easy care, propagation via stem cuttings, no fertilisation required. Also confirms trade names Guppy Grass and Indian Naiad.
